learning_objectives: Translate English statements into symbolic logic notation
Identify propositions and distinguish them from non-propositions
Apply logical connectives: negation, conjunction, disjunction, conditional, biconditional
readings: A Concise Introduction to Logic Ch. 1-2 by Hurley

lecture_notes.introduction: Logic is the study of valid reasoning. It provides the tools to determine whether a conclusion follows necessarily from its premises, regardless of topic. Every field depends on logical reasoning, and this course trains you to do it deliberately and precisely.
key_concepts: Propositions: statements that are true or false
Logical connectives and their symbols: ¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔
Translating everyday language into symbolic form
Truth tables for evaluating compound propositions
Tautologies, contradictions, and contingencies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Translating a news editorial paragraph into symbolic logic and evaluating its logical structure

review.value: ¬p (not p), p ∧ q (p and q), p ∨ q (p or q), p → q (if p then q), p ↔ q (p if and only if q)
List all possible combinations of truth values for variables (2^n rows for n variables), then evaluate the compound proposition column by column

class_schedule.topic: Deductive Reasoning and Valid Argument Forms
learning_objectives: Distinguish between valid and invalid argument forms
Apply modus ponens, modus tollens, and hypothetical syllogism
Test argument validity using truth tables
readings: A Concise Introduction to Logic Ch. 6
Common Valid and Invalid Argument Forms (handout)

lecture_notes.introduction: A valid argument is one where the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises. Validity is about structure, not content. An argument can have false premises and still be valid.
key_concepts: Validity vs. soundness: valid structure vs. valid structure with true premises
Modus ponens: if p then q; p; therefore q
Modus tollens: if p then q; not q; therefore not p
Hypothetical syllogism: if p then q; if q then r; therefore if p then r
Common invalid forms: affirming the consequent, denying the antecedent
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students identify valid and invalid arguments in a set of political and advertising examples

review.value: If it rains, the ground is wet. It is raining. Therefore the ground is wet. Valid: affirming the antecedent.
If it rains, the ground is wet. The ground is wet. Therefore it rained. Invalid: a sprinkler could also wet the ground.

learning_objectives: Evaluate the strength of inductive arguments
Construct and critique arguments by analogy
Distinguish between strong and weak inductive generalizations
readings: A Concise Introduction to Logic Ch. 9
The Art of Thinking Clearly Ch. 1-5 by Rolf Dobelli

lecture_notes.introduction: Most everyday reasoning is inductive, not deductive. Inductive arguments do not guarantee their conclusions but can provide strong support. Understanding what makes an inductive argument stronger or weaker is essential for navigating the real world.
key_concepts: Inductive generalization: from specific cases to a general conclusion
Sample size and representativeness
Argument by analogy: comparing similar cases
Criteria for evaluating analogies: relevance, number, and diversity of similarities
Strengthening and weakening inductive arguments
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating the strength of consumer product reviews as inductive arguments for purchase decisions

review.value: Large, representative sample; relevant similarities; no obvious counterexamples. The conclusion is probable, not certain.
Stronger when: more relevant similarities, fewer relevant differences, conclusion is modest rather than sweeping

learning_objectives: Use heuristics: working backward, finding patterns, simplifying, and drawing diagrams
Recognize when a heuristic is helpful and when it misleads
readings: How to Solve It by George Polya, Part I
Problem Solving Through Recreational Mathematics Ch. 1 by Averbach & Chein

lecture_notes.introduction: Problem solving is a skill that improves with deliberate practice and awareness of strategies. Polya's framework and targeted heuristics give you a toolkit for approaching problems you have never seen before.
key_concepts: Polya's four steps: understand, plan, execute, review
Heuristic: working backward from the goal
Heuristic: looking for patterns in simpler cases
Heuristic: drawing a diagram or table
Heuristic: breaking a complex problem into sub-problems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Solving three classic logic puzzles using different heuristics: river crossing, weighing, and scheduling problems

review.value: Understand the problem (what is given? what is asked?), Devise a plan (choose a strategy), Carry out the plan (execute carefully), Look back (verify and reflect)
Start from the desired result and reverse the steps to find the starting conditions. Especially useful when the goal is clear but the path is not.

learning_objectives: Apply expected value reasoning to real-world choices
Recognize cognitive traps in decision making: sunk cost fallacy, anchoring, framing effects
readings: Thinking, Fast and Slow Ch. 25-29 by Daniel Kahneman

lecture_notes.introduction: Decisions with uncertain outcomes are everywhere: career choices, financial investments, medical treatments. Logic and probability together provide tools for making better decisions when certainty is impossible.
key_concepts: Decision matrix: options, outcomes, probabilities, and values
Expected value as a decision criterion
Risk aversion vs. risk seeking
Sunk cost fallacy: past costs should not affect future decisions
Framing effects: how presentation changes choices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students build decision matrices for a scholarship choice scenario with different risk profiles

review.value: Multiply each outcome's value by its probability, then sum. Choose the option with the highest expected value for rational decisions.
Continuing a failing project because of money already spent. Rational decisions consider only future costs and benefits, not irrecoverable past investments.

learning_objectives: Write step-by-step algorithms in pseudocode for everyday problems
Apply Boolean logic to conditional decision structures
Trace an algorithm's execution to verify correctness
readings: Computational Thinking for the Modern Problem Solver Ch. 1-3
What Is an Algorithm? (Khan Academy supplementary)

lecture_notes.introduction: Algorithmic thinking is the ability to express a solution as a precise sequence of steps. It connects classical logic to modern computation and is valuable whether or not you ever write code.
key_concepts: Algorithms: finite, precise, unambiguous step-by-step procedures
Pseudocode notation for expressing algorithms
Conditional logic: IF-THEN-ELSE structures
Loops: repeating steps until a condition is met
Tracing: stepping through an algorithm manually to verify output
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing and tracing a pseudocode algorithm for making change with the fewest coins

review.value: Must be finite (terminates), definite (each step is precise), effective (each step can be carried out), and produce output
Execute the algorithm by hand with specific input values, tracking variable values at each step to verify the output matches expectations

questions.question: Translate the following argument into symbolic logic and determine if it is valid using a truth table: 'If the economy improves, unemployment drops. The economy improved. Therefore unemployment dropped.'
questions.answer: Let p = 'the economy improves' and q = 'unemployment drops'. The argument is: p → q, p, therefore q. This is modus ponens. Truth table verification: the only row where both premises are true (p=T, q=T) has a true conclusion. There is no row where both premises are true and the conclusion is false. Therefore the argument is valid.

rubric.key: Valid truth table verification or correct validity determination

questions.question: Explain the difference between a valid argument and a sound argument. Give an example of an argument that is valid but not sound.
questions.answer: A valid argument has a structure where the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises; if the premises were true, the conclusion would have to be true. A sound argument is valid AND has all true premises. Example of valid but not sound: 'All birds can fly. Penguins are birds. Therefore penguins can fly.' The structure is valid (modus ponens with universal generalization), but the first premise is false, so the argument is unsound.

rubric.key: Valid but unsound example with explanation

questions.question: You are offered two job options. Job A pays $50,000 with a 90% chance you will enjoy the work. Job B pays $70,000 with a 40% chance you will enjoy the work. Assuming you value job satisfaction at $20,000 per year, which job has the higher expected value? Show your calculation.
questions.answer: Job A: Base pay $50,000. Expected satisfaction bonus = 0.90 × $20,000 = $18,000. Total expected value = $68,000. Job B: Base pay $70,000. Expected satisfaction bonus = 0.40 × $20,000 = $8,000. Total expected value = $78,000. Job B has the higher expected value at $78,000 vs. $68,000. However, a risk-averse person might prefer Job A's higher probability of satisfaction.

rubric.key: Correct expected value calculations for both jobs
Correct identification of higher expected value
Notes risk preference as a consideration

questions.question: Describe the sunk cost fallacy and explain why it leads to irrational decisions. Give a real-world example from everyday life.
questions.answer: The sunk cost fallacy is the tendency to continue investing in something because of previously invested resources (time, money, effort) that cannot be recovered, rather than evaluating only the future costs and benefits. It is irrational because past costs are irrelevant to future outcomes; they are gone regardless of what you decide next. Example: finishing a terrible movie because you paid $15 for the ticket. The $15 is spent whether you stay or leave. The rational choice compares: will the next 90 minutes of this movie bring more value than whatever else I could do with that time?

rubric.key: Correct definition of sunk cost fallacy

major_assignments.title: Logic and Problem Solving Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Create a portfolio demonstrating your mastery of logical reasoning and problem-solving techniques. The portfolio includes three components: a logical analysis of a real-world argument, a solved set of challenge problems using different heuristics, and a decision analysis using a decision matrix for a real choice you face.
objectives: Apply formal logic to evaluate real-world arguments
Demonstrate fluency with multiple problem-solving heuristics
Use decision analysis tools to make a real-world choice
Reflect on your growth as a logical thinker and problem solver
deliverables: Logical analysis (500 words): symbolize and evaluate an argument from a published editorial
Problem-solving showcase: 5 challenge problems solved with documented strategies and reflection
Decision analysis: complete decision matrix for a real personal or academic decision
Reflective introduction (300 words) connecting all three components

criteria: Argument correctly translated to symbolic form
Validity assessed using truth table or recognized form
Clear written explanation of the analysis
At least three different heuristics applied and labeled
Work shown with Polya's framework applied
Reflection on which strategies worked and why
Complete decision matrix with realistic options and outcomes
Thoughtful discussion of risk preferences and non-quantifiable factors
All three portfolio components completed with rigor and depth
Logical analysis demonstrates precise symbolic reasoning
Problem solutions show strategic, documented approaches
Decision analysis applies quantitative tools to a genuine choice

homework.description: Weekly logic exercises and problem sets

quizzes.description: Regular assessment of logical reasoning and problem-solving concepts

portfolio_project.description: Comprehensive portfolio demonstrating logic, problem solving, and decision analysis skills

midterm_exam.description: Covers formal logic, argument validity, and inductive reasoning

final_exam.description: Cumulative exam including logic, problem solving, and decision theory

course_policies.attendance: Class sessions include collaborative problem solving that cannot be replicated individually. More than 3 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade by 3% per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Homework is due at the start of class. Late submissions lose 10% per day. Portfolio components have firm deadlines as each builds on the previous.
course_policies.collaboration: You may discuss logic exercises and problems with classmates but must write up all solutions independently. Identical solutions will be investigated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must represent your own reasoning. Copying solutions from online sources or other students undermines the purpose of the course and will result in a zero on the assignment.
course_policies.technology_requirements: No specialized technology required. A scientific calculator is helpful. Optional: logic simulation software links provided in LMS for extra practice.
